title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Flow is a psychological state (0) in which a person is fully immersed in an activity, losing all sense of time. (9) Although it is often associated with athletes and musicians, anyone can experience flow during a challenging task. (10) Such is the intensity of this focus that people often report feeling a sense of effortless control over their actions. (11) Provided that the difficulty of the task matches the individual's skill level, the conditions for flow are ideal. (12) Not only does this state increase productivity, but it also significantly enhances a person's overall sense of well-being. (13) Regardless of the specific activity involved, the fundamental characteristics of flow remain the same across different cultures. (14) Not until the 1970s did psychologists begin to formally study this phenomenon and its impact on human performance. (15) Since the initial research was published, flow has become a key concept in positive psychology and sports coaching. (16) Whether we can consciously trigger this state or if it occurs purely by chance is still being debated by experts. This profound mental engagement highlights the incredible capacity of the human mind to focus and achieve excellence in many diverse fields.

example.text: Flow is a psychological state (0) in which a person is fully immersed in an activity.
example.explanation: Preposition used with 'which' to refer to a state or condition.

questions.explanation: Conjunction used to introduce a contrast or concession.
Determiner used for emphasis before a noun phrase.
Conjunction used to express a condition.
Part of the 'Not only... but also' structure.
Part of the fixed phrase 'Regardless of'.
Part of the 'Not until' structure requiring inversion ('did psychologists begin').
Conjunction/Preposition indicating a starting point in time.
Conjunction introducing two alternatives or a doubt.
